scenario: A Timer Start Event needs to fire once at a specific datetime (e.g., 2026-06-01T09:00:00Z -- a product launch moment). The team is choosing between Timer Date, Timer Duration, and Timer Cycle types.
question: Which Timer type fits "one-shot at specific datetime"?

options.text: Timer Date type with the ISO 8601 datetime literal: 2026-06-01T09:00:00Z. Fires once at that absolute moment in time. Timer Date is the appropriate choice for a product launch that must trigger at a specific future date and time. The engine schedules the timer to fire at that UTC instant, regardless of when the process instance was created or how long it has been running. FEEL expressions returning a date and time value also work here.

Timer Duration with P0D represents a zero-length duration that fires immediately upon activation, not at any specific future date and time. Duration timers measure elapsed time from the moment the timer activates -- they are relative, not absolute. Using P0D fires the timer at deployment time or process start, making it unsuitable for scheduling a specific product launch moment in the future.

Timer Cycle with R1/2026-06-01T09:00:00Z/PT0S technically fires once at the specified anchor date, but the Cycle type is semantically designed for repeating patterns. Specifying R1 limits it to one repetition, which creates an awkward workaround. The Timer Date type directly and cleanly expresses the intent of a single one-shot absolute datetime fire, making it the canonical choice for the described scenario.

Timer types in Camunda 8 are not interchangeable -- they have distinct semantics that serve different scheduling use cases. Timer Date fires at an absolute moment in time, Timer Duration fires after a relative delay from activation, and Timer Cycle fires repeatedly at specified intervals. Choosing the wrong type will cause incorrect scheduling behaviour, which can result in missing a product launch window or triggering at the wrong time.

a.text: Correct. Camunda 8 Timer Events support three types: Date (absolute moment): one-shot at specified date-time. Use 2026-06-01T09:00:00Z (ISO 8601 datetime with timezone) or FEEL expression returning a date and time value. For "fire at specific moment in the future." Duration (relative): fires after the specified duration from activation. Use PT24H, P7D, etc. For "fire X time after activation." Cycle (repeating): fires repeatedly at the specified interval. Use R/PT1H (unbounded), R10/PT1H (10 times), or include a start anchor. For "fire repeatedly." For "one-shot at specific datetime," Date is the canonical type.

b.text: Incorrect. Duration is relative, not absolute datetime.

c.text: Incorrect. Cycle for one-shot is unnecessary; Date type is direct.
